description: A prolific cascading cherry tomato that is perfect for hanging baskets and tall containers. It produces masses of sweet, red cherry tomatoes throughout the summer. No staking or side-shoot removal needed—just let it tumble!

characteristics.waterNeeds: High — daily watering essential in baskets
characteristics.soilType: Rich potting compost with water-retaining gel

pinchOutTops.info: Not typically required for this plant.

february: 🌱 Sow seeds indoors (plant near sunny windowsill to avoid leggy seedlings)
march: 🌱 Sow seeds indoors (plant near sunny windowsill to avoid leggy seedlings)
april: 🪴 Plant out into final containers (if protected)
october: 🧣 Bring inside or protect from frost

care.sowIndoors: Sow seeds 0.5cm deep in small pots on a warm, sunny windowsill (18–21°C) to avoid leggy seedlings. Keep moist. Seeds sprout in 7–14 days.
care.hardenOff: From mid-May, gradually acclimatise plants to outdoor conditions over 7–10 days.
care.plantOut: After the last frost (mid-May in London), plant 1 plant per 25cm basket or 3 in a 35cm basket. Hang in a sunny, sheltered spot.
care.watering: Hanging baskets dry out very fast! Water daily in summer, twice daily during heatwaves. Inconsistent watering causes split fruit.
care.feeding: Feed weekly with a high-potash tomato fertiliser once the first fruits set.
care.harvest: Pick red fruits regularly to encourage new flowers. The more you pick, the more you get.
care.overwintering: Annual plant — compost at the end of the season.

propagation.seedDescription: Small, flat, pale yellow seeds. Easy to handle.
propagation.cuttingsMethod: Side shoots can be rooted in water, but it's rarely done for basket varieties as you want the bushy growth.

deadheading.notes: No deadheading or pinching out needed. Just remove any yellowing leaves to improve airflow.

seedSaving.method: Squeeze seeds from a ripe fruit into a jar of water. Ferment for 3 days, rinse well, and dry on paper.
seedSaving.seedPodDescription: Seeds are inside the juicy red fruit.

troubleshooting: Drying out — the #1 issue with baskets. Water, water, water!
Blossom end rot — caused by erratic watering.
Aphids — wipe off or spray with soapy water.
funFact: Tumbling Tom was specifically bred to have a cascading habit, making it one of the most popular basket tomatoes in the UK.
